divergences.description: agent_b classifies exposure of CLAUDE/AGENTS operational identifiers as a day-0 blocker, while agent_c prefers split treatment (blocker timing for high-risk identifiers, lower tier for planning/scratch artifacts); agent_a is largely aligned but less explicit on split criteria.

agent_b favors retaining the full 19-issue artifact with detailed sequencing, whereas agent_c emphasizes stricter phase-gated prioritization to keep go/no-go execution manageable; agent_a remains more concise with less long-tail detail.

agent_b keeps more branding/domain coupling in higher urgency buckets, while agent_c limits blocker/critical status to runtime, deployability, or security-impacting coupling and treats attribution-only references as non-blocking.

rationale: agent_b remains strongest on completeness and operational execution detail, and agent_a remains strongest on concise synthesis. Convergence is high on core blockers and remediation direction, but actionable differences persist around internal-doc severity timing, final artifact breadth, and branding-adjacent severity policy.
